text: The incision begins slightly below the mastoid process and continues in a downward and horizontal direction toward the cricoid cartilage.
A vertical arm provides adequate exposure for the dissection of the supraclavicular fossa.
A superior skin flap is raised deep to the platysma muscle, preserving the superficial cervical fascia.
The lateral flap is carefully raised, dissecting the external jugular vein.
The dissection is taken posteriorly as far as the great auricular nerve.
An inferior flap is raised up to the level of the clavicle.
The external jugular vein is ligated and the fascia is elevated from the sternocleidomastoid muscle.
The muscle is completely released from its surrounding fascia.
The spinal accessory nerve is identified as it enters the sternocleidomastoid muscle.
In order to dissect the submandibular lymph nodes, the fascia is incised at the upper boundary of the surgical field, from the midline to the tail of the parotid gland.
The retromandibular vein is ligated and divided.
The submandibular gland is mobilized from its fascial covering, and the facial vein is also divided.
The digastic and stilohyoid muscles are identified in the submandibular fossa.
The submandibular lymph nodes are moved in one piece, preserving the hypoglossal nerve.
The spinal accessory nerve is exposed and the fibro fatty tissue of the space line superior and posterior to the nerve is drawn out in continuity with the main part of the specimen.
Osvaldo Suarez called this step the spinal accessory maneuver.
The motor supply to the trapezius muscle from the cervical plexus should also be preserved.
The sternocleidomastoid muscle is retracted anteriorly, and the fascia from its posterior border is dissected, along with the contents of the supraclavicular fossa.
The external jugular vein is ligated and divided in the posterior triangle of the neck.
Here, the transverse cervical artery and the omohyoid muscle constitute important anatomic landmarks.
The spinal accessory nerve is identified and dissected in the posterior triangle.
The contents of the supraclavicular fossa are passed beneath the muscle and the entire specimen is dissected in a forward direction over the scalene group.
For a complete preservation of shoulder function, the dissection should be carried over the branches of the cervical plexus.
The phrenic nerve is also identified and preserved.
The carotid sheath is now dissected, cutting with the scalpel over the vagus nerve.
The internal jugular vein is completely released from its fascia.
The main branches of the internal jugular vein are ligated and divided.
To complete the isolation of the cervical lymph nodes, the strap muscles are dissected from the covering fascia.
Functional neck dissection is now completed and the remaining neck structures are visible.

Stelohyoid muscle.

spinal accessory nerve and branches from the cervical plexus to the trapezius, phrenic nerve, transverse cervical artery, and omohyoid muscle.
